Teaching Objectives
This course presents students with advanced aspects of communication networks using the top-down approach. The content focuses on the three upper layers: application, transport and network, where the protocols associated with these layers are studied in depth.
Recommended Prerequisites
Fundamental notions related to computer networks
Course Content (Lectures: 21h)
Chapter 1: Communication Paradigms and Application Protocols
Communication models: Client/Server, Peer-to-Peer, Publish/Subscribe
Application protocols: HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SIP, SMTP, DNS, CoAP, MQTT, etc.
VoIP and RoIP: operating principles and associated protocols
SIP: session establishment and management
RTP: real-time audio/video stream transport
Chapter 2: Multiplexing and Transport Protocols
Multiplexing and demultiplexing techniques: source multiplexing and destination demultiplexing, port usage, transport-application layer association
Transport protocols: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol), UDP (User Datagram Protocol), SCTP (Stream Control Transmission Protocol): multi-stream, TCP/UDP hybrid
Chapter 3: Routing and Network Protocols
IPv4 protocol review: IPv4 address structure, subnet masks, private/public addressing
Routing in IP networks: distance vector protocols (e.g. RIP), link state protocols (e.g. OSPF), inter-domain routing (BGP), multicast routing: principles and examples (e.g. PIM)
Assessment
Exam: 60%
Continuous assessment: 40%
Bibliographic References
Kurose, J. F., & Ross, K. W. (2021). Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach. Pearson Editions.
Bonaventure, O., Networking: Principles, Protocols and Practice, 3rd Edition, 2021.
Lannone, E., Telecommunication networks, O'Reilly Edition, 2017.
